description:
You are an on-call operations engineer who has just received an alert that
        “external-dns” traffic might be degraded.  
        To start triage you decide to collect two quick network-diagnostic checks
        and place the results in a single log file so they can be attached to the
        ticketing system.

        Required checks
        1. ICMP reachability to Google’s public resolver 8.8.8.8  
           (only **one** packet is necessary).  
           Command hint: it’s the classic utility that prints a line beginning
           with the word “PING …”.
        2. Forward DNS lookup of “example.com” using any resolver available on
           the machine.  The simplest way is to output only the resource-record
           data (i.e., the IP addresses themselves).

        Required artefacts
        • Directory: /home/user/incidents  
          (create it if it does not yet exist; no elevated privileges required).  
        • File: /home/user/incidents/network_diagnostics.log

        Exact file format
        The file **must contain exactly two lines** in the precise order and
        syntax shown below, followed by a trailing newline character:

        PING_CHECK: PING 8.8.8.8 (8.8.8.8) 56(84) bytes of data.  
        DNS_CHECK: 93.184.216.34, 2606:2800:220:1:248:1893:25c8:1946

        Notes on formatting rules
        • “PING_CHECK:” and “DNS_CHECK:” must appear in UPPER-CASE followed by a
          single space.
        • The rest of each line is case-sensitive and must match exactly; do not
          add extra spaces or tabs.
        • The second line must list the two expected IP addresses separated by
          a comma+space, nothing more.
        • Do not include timestamps or any additional text before or after the
          two required lines.
        • Ensure a final newline (ASCII LF) after the second line so linters and
          automated tests do not fail.

        What the automated grader will look for
        1. Presence of /home/user/incidents/network_diagnostics.log  
        2. The file contains two lines whose contents match the specification
           byte-for-byte.
        3. Correct permissions for a regular user-owned file (the agent runs as
           “user”; default umask is fine).

        Your terminal session should:
        • Run the two diagnostics (2–3 commands total is plenty).  
        • Place the captured text into the log file in the format given.  
        • Optionally cat the file at the end so a human can quickly inspect it.

        Remember:
        ─ No sudo/root privileges are available.  
        ─ Keep your command sequence short and readable.  
        ─ The grader cares only about the final file and its exact content.
truth:
Expected artefact for verification:

        File path:
        /home/user/incidents/network_diagnostics.log

        Exact byte-for-byte content (including terminating newline):

        PING_CHECK: PING 8.8.8.8 (8.8.8.8) 56(84) bytes of data.
        DNS_CHECK: 93.184.216.34, 2606:2800:220:1:248:1893:25c8:1946

        (Each line ends with '\n'; the file therefore contains 2 newline characters.)
name: task_000000_e9b09f56
